Dear senior physicist,
 
      This letter from The Molecular Sciences Institute. The reason is to try to recruit a few more postdoctoral fellows trained in experimental physics.  These physicists will, we hope, help devise new methods to gather biological information, and help make knowledge out of  the results. I imagine such people could have trained in many different kinds of physics, so, rather than imagine fields, I'll say we're looking for the brightest and will commit to trying to fill any gaps in education they might have--which in any case will be mainly be biological.

       About the Institute.  It's new.  Its mission is to speed the development of genomic  technologies and analytical and computational frameworks to enable prediction of the  behavior of biological systems.  It's thriving. The mix of biologists, computationalists, and physicists is intellectually and sociologically challenging.  Boundaries between the labs (at the moment, mine and Sydney Brenner's) are blurring; and the place has already clearly  emerged as a high-independence postdoctoral training environment. The existing cadre of research fellows is superb.  Relations with UCSF and UC Berkeley are good and deepening nicely.  Although the labs (designed by Ken Kornberg) are lovely, we still plan to move to the UCSF Mission Bay development in 2002.

      In deciding to start this institute, we had to bet that this was the right time try to  integrate computation and biology, and to try to create new methods to capture genomic information.  Gratifyingly, in the past year, the biological community has pretty much gotten religion on these topics.  From the top of the establishment all the way down, all auguries are that the commitment to try to take biology to the next step will be a lasting one.  So will be the commitment to bright people initially trained in other disciplines.

     A move from physics to biology is obviously a large step, and it's probably not the right step for most physics students.  But, I would argue that to a great extent measurement is measurement, that some of the problems thrown up by the progress of the genome projects are terribly important, and that beyond these there are likely to be deep truths to be found in biology.  For the right young person, a decision to study living systems may be an opportunity to contribute greatly to human knowledge.

    So I hope you will be willing to post this, and to encourage those of your students who might be appropriate to apply.
 

Best regards,
 

Roger Brent
 
 
 

Ongoing and contemplated research areas at TMSI
 

Assays for protein interactions in vivo.

Interactions among evolutionarily conserved protein motifs.

Protein based recognition molecules.

Biological and physical probes for cellular events.

Functional genomic instrumentation.

Computational evolutionary biology.

Mathematical frameworks for simulation of cell function.

Directed selection and quantification of fitness in populations of evolving model organisms.

Computational tools to extract functional data from genomic information.
